title: Why Did CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV) Stock Move Down -11.38% Today?
author: tickeron_community
selftext:
# Key Takeaways

* Shares of [CRWV](https://tickeron.com/ticker/CRWV/) declined approximately 11.38% in Friday's session, falling from a prior close of $97.63 to around $86.52, after the company reported Q4 2025 earnings after the bell on Thursday, February 26
* The primary catalyst was a wider-than-expected net loss of $0.56 per share on EPS expectations of −$0.49, alongside a massive capex plan calling for $30–$35 billion in infrastructure spending in 2026, more than doubling the prior year
* Q1 2026 revenue guidance of $1.9–$2.0 billion fell short of the $2.29 billion analysts had projected, adding to near-term pressure on the stock
* Despite a revenue beat for Q4 ($1.57 billion vs. estimates of $1.53–$1.55 billion) and a $66.8 billion contracted backlog, investors focused on ballooning losses and cash burn
* Full-year 2026 revenue guidance of $12–$13 billion came in roughly in line with the $12.09 billion consensus, offering little upside comfort
* Traders are closely watching margin recovery timelines, capex execution, and whether CoreWeave can convert its unprecedented backlog into profitability

# Opening Summary

CoreWeave, Inc. ([CRWV](https://tickeron.com/ticker/CRWV/)) is a Nasdaq-listed AI cloud infrastructure provider specializing in GPU-accelerated computing, primarily serving hyperscalers, AI-native companies, and enterprise clients with high-performance workloads. Shares fell approximately 11.38% on Friday, February 27, declining from the prior closing price of $97.63 to approximately $86.52 in early trading. The selloff followed the company's after-hours Q4 2025 earnings release, which revealed a net loss per share that exceeded analyst estimates and featured a capital expenditure projection for 2026 that more than doubled the prior year's figure, unnerving investors despite a headline revenue beat.

# Earnings Miss on the Bottom Line

CoreWeave reported Q4 2025 earnings per share of −$0.56, falling short of the Wall Street consensus of −$0.49, a meaningful miss that highlighted the accelerating cost burden of rapid infrastructure expansion.  The company's Q4 net loss reached $452 million, compared to a $51 million net loss in Q4 2024, driven largely by interest expenses that surged to $388 million from $149 million a year earlier.  While adjusted EBITDA for the quarter came in at $898 million with a 57% margin, GAAP profitability remains elusive and deteriorating as the company races to deploy capacity ahead of customer revenue ramp.

# $30 Billion Capex Shock

The single most impactful number from the earnings release was CoreWeave's 2026 capital expenditure guidance of $30–$35 billion, a figure that more than doubled the company's 2025 levels.  Management explained that costs are recognized immediately when new data center capacity comes online, while revenue from those deployments ramps over subsequent months, creating a significant near-term earnings gap.  This front-loaded investment model raises legitimate questions about free cash flow and dilution risk, even as management expressed confidence that the company is "virtually sold out" through 2026.

# Guidance Disappointment on Q1 Revenue

CoreWeave guided Q1 2026 revenue to between $1.9 billion and $2.0 billion, notably below the $2.29 billion that analysts tracked by LSEG had anticipated.  Full-year 2026 revenue guidance of $12–$13 billion was roughly in line with the $12.09 billion consensus, but offered no meaningful upside surprise to offset the earnings miss and capex escalation.  Management also flagged that Q1 2026 adjusted operating income would range from $0 to $40 million, with margins expected to trough in the first quarter before recovering to low double digits by Q4 and eventually reaching a 25–30% long-term target.

# The Backlog Narrative vs. Near-Term Reality

On the positive side, CoreWeave reported a contracted revenue backlog of $66.8 billion and full-year 2025 revenue of $5.1 billion, up 168% year-over-year, with Q4 revenue of $1.57 billion exceeding analyst estimates of $1.53–$1.55 billion.  Management pointed to multi-year contract extensions and broadening demand from hyperscalers as evidence of structural tailwinds for AI infrastructure.  However, the gap between backlog scale and near-term profitability left many investors unconvinced, as the stock's reaction demonstrated that execution risk and cash burn are the market's current primary concern.

# Market Context and Trading Activity

Friday's trading volume in [CRWV](https://tickeron.com/ticker/CRWV/) was elevated, consistent with heavy post-earnings activity.  The broader technology sector and AI-adjacent names saw mixed action, but [CRWV](https://tickeron.com/ticker/CRWV/)'s decline was considerably more pronounced than sector peers, reflecting company-specific rather than macro-driven selling.  From a technical standpoint, the drop pushed shares significantly below the 50-day moving average of approximately $86.16–$86.65, a key support level that, if lost on a closing basis, could invite additional technical selling pressure.  The stock's 52-week range of $33.52–$187.00 illustrates just how volatile [CRWV](https://tickeron.com/ticker/CRWV/) has been since its IPO.

# What Comes Next for CRWV

With Q1 2026 expected to be the trough quarter for margins, investors will be watching closely whether CoreWeave can execute on its infrastructure build-out and deliver a sequential improvement in adjusted operating income through the year.  The company has outlined an ambitious long-term revenue trajectory, targeting annualized run-rate revenues of $17–$19 billion by end of 2026 and more than $30 billion by end of 2027.  Outstanding risks include ongoing securities fraud class action litigation — with a lead plaintiff deadline of March 13, 2026 — significant insider selling over recent months, and execution uncertainty around deploying $30+ billion in capital with the expected returns.  Analyst consensus remains a "Buy" with a price target of approximately $124–$125, but multiple firms have already moved to cut their targets following Friday's reaction, and further estimate revisions are likely in the days ahead.  The Q1 2026 earnings report will be the next critical data point to assess whether the margin recovery narrative is on track.
